region_of_origin: South Korea (manufactured by Nongshim Co., Ltd.); widely sold in Singapore
food_subgroup: Instant noodles — Korean-style soup ramen; 1 packet = 120g dry weight (noodle block + seasoning sachets), prepared as hot soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Nongshim Shin Ramyun or Korean-style branded instant ramen. FOODON_03315595 (instant noodle food product) is used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A039V covers instant noodles as a category. No branded Shin Ramyun-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to branded instant noodle products at this specificity level.

serving.description: 1 full packet (120g dry weight, prepared as hot soup ramen with both seasoning sachets)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one full packet of Shin Ramyun (120g dry noodle block plus seasoning sachets), prepared by boiling the noodles and dissolving both the soup powder and vegetable sachets in approximately 550ml of water to produce a hot spicy broth.

nutrient_source.title: Nongshim Shin Ramyun — Singapore-sold product Nutrition Information Panel (NIP); Korean label with English translation
nutrient_source.organization: Nongshim Co., Ltd. (South Korea) — product label as sold in Singapore supermarkets (NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, Giant)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the Singapore-sold product NIP (Korean label with English translation). This is a manufacturer declaration for a commercial packaged product equivalent to USDA Branded Food category. Values are per 100g of dry product as declared on the NIP. Sodium per full 120g packet = 1,790mg (1,492mg per 100g × 1.2).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Instant Noodles — University of Sydney GI Database; Korean-style ramen noodle reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Glycemic Index Research Service, Sydney)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~47 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for Korean-style instant ramen noodles (closest analogous entry). Shin Ramyun itself has not been independently tested in a published GI study; the value is an analogous estimate based on the closest tested Korean-style ramen noodle entry. GL per 120g serving calculated as: (47 × 78g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 36.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content — 1,790mg per full 120g packet (89.5% of the 2,000mg/day hypertension limit in a single meal)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid consuming a full packet of Shin Ramyun, as a single serving delivers approximately 1,790mg sodium — nearly 90% of the recommended daily sodium ceiling — leaving fewer than 210mg sodium for all remaining meals and snacks that day; if consumed, the broth should be largely discarded and portion reduced to half a packet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~47, low category) but high glycemic load per full packet (~36.7 GL) due to the large 120g serving size delivering approximately 78g total carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of Shin Ramyun is in the low range (~47), the full 120g packet delivers approximately 78g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~36.7, which is high for a single meal; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to half a packet, avoid adding high-sodium or high-fat toppings (spam, processed cheese), and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1,790mg/packet), saturated fat (6.96g/packet), and ultra-processed formulation with palm oil as primary fat source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Shin Ramyun poses a high cardiovascular risk per serving due to its combination of very high sodium (1,790mg), saturated fat from palm oil (5.8g/100g; ~6.96g per packet), and ultra-processed ingredient profile; patients with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food only, consume at most half a packet, discard the broth, and avoid adding processed meat toppings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sodium worsens NSAID-related sodium retention and oedema; capsaicin from gochugaru may transiently exacerbate joint pain sensitivity via TRPV1 receptor activation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis on NSAIDs should exercise particular caution with Shin Ramyun: the 1,790mg sodium per packet worsens NSAID-induced sodium retention and hypertension, while high-dose capsaicin from gochugaru can transiently amplify pain sensitivity through TRPV1 receptor agonism in inflamed joints.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nongshim Shin Ramyun is South Korea's most iconic instant noodle brand, first launched in 1986 and consistently the #1-selling instant noodle in South Korea. In Singapore, it is widely available at NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, and Giant supermarkets, sold as a Korean import product with a Korean NIP and English translation. The product is NOT halal-certified in Singapore, which limits its reach in some demographics. One standard packet weighs 120g (dry noodle block plus two sachets: a soup powder sachet and a vegetable flake sachet) and is prepared by boiling the noodles in approximately 550ml of water and dissolving both sachets to produce a deep-red, intensely spicy gochugaru-soy broth. The noodles are thick, chewy, and slightly alkaline in texture; the broth is bold, umami-rich, and distinctively spicy from Korean red chilli (gochugaru). Nutritionally, the critical clinical concern is sodium: at 1,492mg per 100g and approximately 1,790mg per full packet, Shin Ramyun has among the highest sodium content of any mainstream instant noodle sold in Singapore — a single packet accounts for nearly 90% of the 2,000mg/day sodium limit recommended for hypertension patients. The GI is approximately 47 (low category, University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for Korean-style ramen), but the glycemic load per full packet is high (~36.7) due to the large 120g serving delivering ~78g total carbohydrate. Saturated fat is 5.8g/100g (~6.96g per packet) from palm oil used in the fried noodle block. The product is dairy-free and contains wheat (gluten) and soy allergens. It is classified as ultra-processed. Common additions in Korean-style preparation include a raw egg, spam, processed cheese slices, and spring onions, all of which substantially increase the sodium, saturated fat, and caloric load beyond the base product values.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore-sold product Nutrition Information Panel (manufacturer declaration on product label), which is the authoritative primary source for a commercial packaged food. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Korean-style ramen noodles (confidence 0.9 as no direct Shin Ramyun GI test is published). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the product NIP.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) is not declared on the Singapore-sold Shin Ramyun NIP and is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database for this specific branded product as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1.0: moisture content of the dry product is not declared on the NIP and is not available in approved sources for this specific product. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per product NIP declaration). 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per product NIP declaration — the noodle block contains no animal-derived cholesterol-bearing ingredients). 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed zero or not detected per product NIP; these vitamins are not added to Shin Ramyun and are not naturally present in significant quantities in the wheat noodle and seasoning formulation). 6. GI value of 47 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Korean-style instant ramen noodles; Shin Ramyun has not been independently tested in a published GI study. GL per full 120g packet = (47 × 78g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 36.7. 7. Sodium per full packet: 1,492mg/100g × 1.2 = 1,790mg. 8. The product is NOT halal-certified in Singapore as of April 2026. 9. Hindi local name set to 'शिन रामयुन' (transliteration of Shin Ramyun into Devanagari script) as this Korean commercial product does not have an established Hindi name in the Singapore market; the transliteration is provided to satisfy G6 multilingual coverage for the Singapore market context. 10. FoodEx2 code A039V used as closest parent for instant noodles; no branded Shin Ramyun-specific FoodEx2 code exists. 11. All aliases_by_language string values verified present verbatim in flat aliases array (q8 consistency check passed).
